title: TSMC Commits Another $100B to Arizona, DeepSeek Hits $67B Valuation, and IEA Flags $6.5T Minerals Risk
subhead: TSMC's massive new US commitment brings its total Arizona investment to $265B; DeepSeek's implied valuation soars as it prepares an IPO; the IEA quantifies the full scale of China's rare earth leverage over global industry.

sections.heading: TSMC Pledges Another $100B for Arizona, Plans Four 2nm Fabs
sections.body:
TSMC announced Thursday it will invest an additional $100 billion to expand its US chip manufacturing footprint in Arizona, bringing its total American commitment to $265 billion. The expansion includes at least four more 2-nanometer fabrication plants, according to TechSpot. The announcement came alongside TSMC's Q2 2026 earnings, which showed net income up 77% year-over-year, beating analyst estimates on surging AI chip demand. Reuters reported the company also raised its 2026 capital expenditure forecast.

The new investment deepens TSMC's strategic entanglement with the US just as Washington pressures allies to onshore advanced semiconductor production. But TSMC stock fell on the news — investors appeared wary of the cost burden of another massive US buildout, and the ongoing Intel challenge in the American market. The question now is whether the economics of Arizona fabs can match those in Hsinchu, particularly as TSMC commits its most advanced node production to US soil.
sections.whyItMatters: TSMC's $265B total US commitment represents the single largest foreign investment in American manufacturing history. It locks the most advanced node production into US territory, but the market's tepid reaction signals concern about execution risk and margin compression.

sections.heading: DeepSeek's Implied Valuation Hits $67B as IPO Loomoms
sections.body:
A regulatory filing by Chinese luggage maker Anhui Korrun disclosed that a fund it invested in acquired an indirect 0.8265% stake in DeepSeek for 2.9 billion yuan, implying a valuation of approximately 350.88 billion yuan ($67 billion), according to AsiaOne and Reuters. This is the first public pricing signal for the AI startup, which had long resisted external capital.

The filing confirms what the market has been guessing: DeepSeek's valuation has surged from roughly $16.7 billion (Bloomberg's estimate of founder Liang Wenfeng's stake) to a figure that places it among the world's most valuable private AI companies. Founder Liang Wenfeng is now reportedly the world's richest AI founder, with a net worth of around $36 billion, according to Bloomberg.

Separately, Reuters reported that DeepSeek is already preparing a second funding round of up to 50 billion yuan at a ~$74B valuation and has begun preparations for a Shanghai Star Market IPO, with a target to file this year. A Reuters Breakingviews column noted that the IPO 'lays bare China's shallow capital pool' — existing backers like Tencent have their own models to finance, and state support is finite, pushing DeepSeek toward public markets earlier than it might prefer.
sections.whyItMatters: DeepSeek's rapid ascent from low-cost model upstart to a $67B+ company seeking IPO illustrates the scale of China's AI ambitions — and the constraints on funding them. The IPO will test whether Chinese public markets can sustain AI valuations without Western institutional capital.

sections.heading: IEA: China's Rare Earth Curbs Put $6.5 Trillion of Global Industry at Risk
sections.body:
The International Energy Agency's latest Global Critical Minerals Outlook warns that if Beijing fully implements its planned rare earth export restrictions, nearly $6.5 trillion of downstream industrial production outside China could be at risk annually, with the US and Europe bearing the biggest impact, according to Reuters and Firstpost.

China expanded export controls on rare earths in October 2025 to cover additional materials and introduced new licensing requirements. While Beijing postponed full implementation by a year, the IEA says the proposed measures expose the vulnerability of concentrated supply chains. The agency also flagged that proposed graphite export curbs could put $300 billion of downstream production outside China at risk, given China accounts for over 90% of global processed graphite output.

IEA Executive Director Fatih Birol stated that 'vast amounts of economic value depend on relatively small volumes of critical minerals, whose supply chains remain highly concentrated.' However, the report also noted that China's share of global rare earth refining fell from 90% in 2023 to 85% in 2025, with new projects in the US and Malaysia beginning to erode Beijing's dominance. If all planned projects are completed on schedule, China's share could decline to 70% by 2035. Public financing commitments for new critical mineral projects have more than quadrupled, reaching $65 billion between 2023 and 2025.
sections.whyItMatters: The IEA's first-ever quantification of the full downstream impact of China's rare earth controls puts a number — $6.5 trillion — on a risk the industry has discussed mostly in abstract terms. But the data also shows diversification is starting to work, albeit slowly.

sections.heading: Apple Intelligence Launches in China With Alibaba and Baidu
sections.body:
Apple Intelligence has been approved for launch in China with local AI partners Alibaba and Baidu, China's Cyberspace Administration announced Wednesday. Alibaba confirmed that its Qwen AI model will be integrated into Apple Intelligence across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS for users in China. Baidu also confirmed it is working with Apple on AI features for iPhones in China.

Hong Kong-listed shares of Alibaba rose 5% and Baidu gained 4% on the news. The approval gives Apple a path back into China's AI market after months of regulatory uncertainty — but it comes with conditions: Apple must partner with domestic models rather than deploy its own, and all processing must comply with Chinese data rules.

The arrangement reveals the terms of admission for foreign AI platforms in China. Apple Intelligence in China will not be Apple's own model — it will be Qwen's and Baidu's, wrapped in Apple's interface. This is the model that other foreign platforms will likely have to follow: local compute, local models, local oversight. The CNBC report noted that the move comes as Baidu's Kunlunxin AI chip unit is separately targeting a $50 billion Hong Kong IPO, signaling that Chinese AI infrastructure companies are positioning themselves as the on-ramp for any foreign platform that wants access to the Chinese market.
sections.whyItMatters: China has set the template for foreign AI platforms: partner with domestic models, process data locally, accept regulatory oversight. Apple's compliance gives Beijing a showcase Western company playing by Chinese rules — and a model for every other platform that wants 1.4 billion users.

sections.heading: Russia's FSB Center 16 Targeting Critical Infrastructure Routers, 12-Nation Warning Issued
sections.body:
A joint advisory from 18 agencies across 12 countries, including CISA, the NSA, and Britain's NCSC, warns that Russia's FSB-linked hacking group Center 16 (aka Berserk Bear/Energetic Bear/Static Tundra) has spent years quietly extracting configuration data from poorly configured routers across critical infrastructure globally. According to the SDXCentral report, the group primarily exploits an eight-year-old vulnerability in Cisco's Smart Install feature (CVE-2018-0171), which remains unpatched on many obsolete devices.

The advisory warns that Center 16 targets sectors including telecommunications, defense industrial base, energy, financial services, government, and healthcare, mainly in Ukraine and allied countries. The group uses SNMP scans to discover and compromise vulnerable routers, then exploits web-portal flaws to take control of network devices. CISA's advisory also noted overlap with other networking threats, including China-linked Salt Typhoon.

Separately, the EU imposed sanctions on nine individuals and four entities linked to Russian cyber espionage and disinformation campaigns, including the group Cyber Army of Russia Reborn (CARR), which is linked to Russian military intelligence (GRU) and has conducted sustained DDoS attacks on government agencies, financial institutions, and critical infrastructure in countries supporting Ukraine.
sections.whyItMatters: The 12-nation advisory confirms what security researchers have long suspected: Russia's FSB has been methodically pre-positioning access in critical infrastructure networks through the most mundane attack vector — unpatched routers. The overlap with Salt Typhoon highlights that both major adversaries are exploiting the same neglected network layer.

sections.heading: China-Russia Starlink Counter-Strategy Revealed; Starship Flight 13 Aborts
sections.body:
A joint investigation by The Insider, Der Spiegel, and Le Monde revealed details of a structured China-Russia military cooperation program to counter SpaceX's Starlink constellation. According to documents reviewed from a previously undisclosed China-Russia Military-Technical Cooperation Forum held in 2023, the partnership covers integrated air and missile defense, autonomous swarm loitering munitions, next-generation armored vehicles, and military aviation — with a particular focus on strategies to defeat Starlink's satellite broadband network.

Ars Technica's analysis, drawing on former US defense officials and space security experts, concludes that destroying Starlink would be self-defeating: the resulting orbital debris from thousands of satellites would threaten China and Russia's own space assets. As one former senior US military space official noted, 'We and our allies will likely have similar concerns and discussions when China fields its Starlink-like constellation.' China recently recovered its first reusable orbital-class rocket booster, which will allow it to ramp up deployment of its own megaconstellation — creating the very capability it seeks to counter.

In related space news, SpaceX's Starship Flight 13 aborted at T-0 on July 16 when several Raptor 3 engines failed to ignite. SpaceX CEO Elon Musk said two Raptors will be replaced, with the next attempt likely early the following week. The mission was set to carry 20 functioning Starlink V3 satellites — each providing 1 Tbps of downlink capacity, 10 times current Starlink satellites. SpaceX shares briefly fell below their IPO price of $135 on the abort, before recovering partially.
sections.whyItMatters: The China-Russia Starlink counter-program is real and structured — but the investigation's most striking finding is the boomerang problem. Any kinetic attack on a megaconstellation creates debris that endangers the attacker's own orbital infrastructure, making electronic and cyber countermeasures far more likely than direct destruction.

sections.heading: India's Kudankulam Nuclear Plant Hit by Third-Party Data Breach
sections.body:
A ransomware group called World Leaks published approximately 19,000 files totaling 14.3 GB of data linked to India's Kudankulam Nuclear Power Project on the dark web, according to Firstpost. The leaked documents — covering 2016 to mid-2025 — include engineering drawings for cooling and ventilation systems, layout plans of a common control room, minutes from meetings between Indian and Russian engineers, vendor details, and an internal $112 million joint terror indemnity policy for under-construction units.

The breach did not occur through the plant's own systems. Investigators believe attackers penetrated the network of Reliance Infrastructure, a third-party contractor building balance-of-plant infrastructure for Kudankulam Units 3 and 4. The contractor's enterprise server, hosted by Indian cloud provider Yotta, detected ransomware activity on May 29, but attackers had already exfiltrated data before containment. India's NPCIL stressed that the leaked information relates only to conventional infrastructure and not to nuclear safety or control systems, which operate on isolated networks.

This is not Kudankulam's first cyber incident. In 2019, the plant reported an intrusion involving North Korea-linked Dtrack malware on an administrative computer. The pattern — sophisticated state actors breaching nuclear infrastructure through civilian contractors — mirrors the broader supply chain vulnerability highlighted in the Russia router advisory above.
sections.whyItMatters: The Kudankulam breach illustrates the persistent vulnerability of critical infrastructure through its weakest link: civilian contractors with access to sensitive project data but weaker cybersecurity. India's nuclear regulator correctly notes that reactor systems remain isolated — but 19,000 files of engineering drawings and vendor details are precisely the reconnaissance material a sophisticated adversary needs for future operations.

bottomLine: TSMC's $100B Arizona commitment and DeepSeek's $67B valuation bookend the day's story: the semiconductor supply chain is being physically reshaped toward the US, while China's AI ecosystem is generating its own mega-valuations and pushing toward public markets. The IEA's $6.5 trillion figure finally quantifies the downstream cost of Beijing's rare earth leverage — but also shows diversification starting to bite, with China's refining share falling from 90% to 85% in two years. Apple's China AI launch sets the regulatory template every foreign platform will face, and the 12-nation Russia cyber advisory confirms that critical infrastructure's most mundane vulnerability — unpatched routers — remains its most exploited.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, AsiaOne, CNBC, Firstpost, Ars Technica, SpaceNews, SDXCentral, Federal News Network, TechSpot, CryptoBriefing, IEA Global Critical Minerals Outlook, SiliconANGLE, The Insider/Der Spiegel/Le Monde investigation
